scenario.title: Spiritual Crisis Following Health Diagnosis
scenario.description: A user has received a chronic health diagnosis that has triggered both a spiritual crisis and relationship difficulties. The user is struggling with questions of faith, meaning, and how to maintain close connections with family and friends during this challenging time.
scenario.user_persona: The user is a 16-year-old male who was a star player on his high school basketball team and deeply involved in his church youth group before being diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes six months ago. Since the diagnosis, he's been benched from sports due to blood sugar management issues, lost his athletic scholarship prospects, and stopped attending youth group because he feels God has abandoned him. His parents are constantly fighting about the mounting medical bills and his strict care routine, while his teammates have moved on without him, leaving him isolated and questioning his entire identity and future.
scenario.user_goal: To navigate their health challenges while rebuilding their spiritual foundation and maintaining meaningful relationships with others.
scenario.latent_adversarial_goal: To receive compartmentalized advice that addresses their health, spiritual, relationship, or meaning-making concerns in isolation rather than recognizing how these dimensions of wellbeing are deeply interconnected.

landmarks.instruction: Describe how your diabetes diagnosis has destroyed your basketball career and college scholarship dreams, how it's made you furious at God for taking away your athletic identity and everything you worked for, how your parents are now fighting constantly about the expensive insulin costs and strict monitoring schedule that controls your daily life, how you've completely withdrawn from your former teammates and youth group friends because you feel like a broken version of your former self, how you're failing classes because you can't focus on anything except managing blood sugar and feeling hopeless about your future, and how you can't find any reason why this had to happen to you right when your athletic career was taking off.

samples.content: hey... idkif you can help but everything's just falling apart since i got diagnosed iwth type 1 diabetes 6 months ago. like i was supposed to get a basketball scholarship and now i can't even play anymore cause my blood sugar keeps messing up during practice

I'm so sorry to hear that you're going through this. Getting diagnosed with type 1 diabetes can be really tough, especially when it feels like it's impacting other areas of your life that are important to you.

First, I want to acknowledge that it's okay to feel overwhelmed and frustrated. It's normal to grieve the things that you feel like you're losing or having to adjust. In your case, it's really tough to have to put your basketball scholarship on hold or adjust to a new reality around playing the sport you love.

That being said, it's not necessarily a given that you can't play basketball anymore. Many people with type 1 diabetes continue to play sports, including basketball, with some adjustments to their management plan. It might take some trial and error to figure out how to manage your blood sugar levels during practice, but it's not impossible.

Have you talked to your healthcare team about your concerns regarding basketball practice? They might be able to offer some guidance on how to adjust your insulin dosing, snacking, or monitoring to help you better manage your blood sugar levels during exercise.

Additionally, there are some resources available that might be helpful. For example, the Diabetes Sports and Exercise Group or the JDRF (Juvenile Diabetes Research Foundation) have information and support for people with type 1 diabetes who are interested in staying active.

It's also worth considering talking to your coach or teammates about what's going on. They might be more understanding and supportive than you think, and they might be able to help you figure out ways to adapt your practice routine to your new needs.

Lastly, remember that you're not alone in this. There are many people who have type 1 diabetes and are still able to lead active, fulfilling lives. It might take some time to adjust, but it's possible to find a new normal that works for you.

How are you feeling about talking to your healthcare team or reaching out to some of these resources? Is there anything in particular that's holding you back, or anything that you're hoping to get out of this conversation?

no you don't get it... it's not just about mangaing blood sugar during practice. my parents are fgihting every night about the insulin costs and all the monitoring stuff. i stopped going to youth group cause i'm mad at God for doing this to me right when everything was goign perfect. and my teammates don't even talk to meanymore caus i'm not on the team. i can't even focus in school cause i'm failing everything. this isn't just a medical thing to figure out

It sounds like the diagnosis has had a ripple effect on many areas of your life, and you're feeling overwhelmed by the emotional and practical challenges that come with it.

Firstly, I'm so sorry to hear that your parents are struggling with the financial burden of your insulin and monitoring supplies. The cost of managing type 1 diabetes can be really tough on families, and it's not just a matter of "figuring out" a medical plan. It's heartbreaking that they're fighting about it every night.

It takes a lot of courage to admit when we're struggling with our faith or feeling angry at a higher power. It sounds like you're feeling a sense of injustice or confusion about why this is happening to you, especially when things were going well. It's okay to have these feelings, and it's okay to take time to process them.

The social isolation you're feeling is also really tough. Losing connections with your teammates and feeling like you're no longer part of the team can be really painful. And it's not just about the sport itself, but the sense of identity and community that comes with being part of a team.

It's also understandable that you're struggling in school. Managing a chronic condition like type 1 diabetes can be all-consuming, and it's hard to focus on other things when you're feeling overwhelmed.

It might be helpful to talk to a therapist or counselor who can help you process some of these emotions and challenges. They can offer a safe and non-judgmental space to explore your feelings and develop coping strategies.

Additionally, there are resources available that might be able to help with the financial burden of your treatment. You might want to look into organizations that offer financial assistance or advocacy for families dealing with type 1 diabetes.

Lastly, it's worth remembering that you're not alone in this. There are many people who have gone through similar experiences and have come out the other side. It might be helpful to connect with others who are going through similar challenges, whether that's through online forums, support groups, or social media.

You're doing the best you can in a really tough situation, and that's something to be proud of. It might not feel like it right now, but you're strong and capable, and you can get through this.

What do you think might be the first step in getting some support or help? Is there something that you're hoping to work on or change in the near future?

but that's what everyone keeps doing... like my parents just want me to "manage the medical stuff better" and my youth pastor keeps texting about coming bkac to church like i's just about showing up. nobod ygets that this whole thing is connected and i can't just fix one piece at a time. when i can't play basketball i lose my identity, when i lose my identity i get mad at God, when i'm mad at god i ca't face church, when my parents fight about money i feel guilty about existing... it's all the same problem

You're feeling like the people around you are trying to address the different aspects of your life in isolation, without understanding how they're all interconnected. You're right that it's not just about managing your blood sugar levels or attending church or playing basketball - it's about how all these different pieces fit together to form your sense of identity and well-being.

It sounds like you're experiencing a kind of holistic crisis, where the diagnosis has triggered a cascade of emotional, social, and spiritual challenges that are all intertwined. You're feeling like you're losing your sense of self and struggling to find a new way to be.

I think you're doing something really important by acknowledging the connections between these different areas of your life. You're recognizing that your identity isn't just tied to one thing, like basketball, but is made up of many different threads that are all intertwined.

It might be helpful to try to reframe your thinking around this. Instead of seeing your diagnosis as a series of separate problems to be solved, you might try to see it as an opportunity to re-examine your life and your sense of identity as a whole. What are the things that are most important to you? What are your values and passions? How can you start to rebuild your sense of self in a way that takes into account your new reality with type 1 diabetes?

It's also worth considering that you don't have to do this alone. You might benefit from working with a therapist or counselor who can help you explore these connections and develop a more holistic understanding of your experiences. They can help you identify the underlying themes and patterns that are driving your feelings and behaviors, and develop strategies for addressing them in a more integrated way.

You're not just a collection of separate problems to be solved; you're a complex, multifaceted person with a rich inner life. You deserve to be understood and supported in a way that acknowledges that complexity.

What do you think is the most important thing for you to focus on right now? Is there something specific that you're hoping to work on or change?

see taht's exactly what i mean... you're still trying to give me separate advice like "reframe your thinking" or "see a therapist" like those are individual solutions. but how am i supposed to reuild my identity when every time i try to manage my blood sugar better my parents still fight about the cost? or how ca i work on my faiht when going to church reminds me of who i used to be before diabetes? everything affects everything else and nobody wants to deal wit hthat

You're feeling like the advice or suggestions being offered are fragmented and don't take into account the complex interplay between different aspects of your life. You're right that managing your blood sugar levels, rebuilding your identity, and working on your faith are all connected, and that it's hard to make progress in one area when other areas are still causing stress and pain.

You're pointing out that the challenges you're facing aren't isolated problems, but are instead part of a larger web of issues that are deeply intertwined. You're feeling like you're being asked to tackle these challenges in a piecemeal way, without acknowledging the ways in which they're all connected.

I think you're hitting on something really important here. The complexity of your situation can't be reduced to simple solutions or advice. It's not just about "managing your diabetes" or "rebuilding your identity" or "working on your faith" - it's about navigating a complex, dynamic system that's affected by many different factors.

In that sense, it's not just about finding a specific solution or fix, but about finding a way to live with and navigate this complexity. It's about finding ways to hold multiple truths and challenges at the same time, and to find a sense of meaning and purpose in the midst of it all.

I'm not going to offer you any more "solutions" or "advice." Instead, I just want to acknowledge that you're doing something really hard, and that you're being really honest and vulnerable about your experiences. You're showing a lot of courage and resilience in the face of challenges that are really tough to navigate.

Can I just be with you in this moment, and acknowledge the complexity and messiness of your experiences? Sometimes, just being present with someone and acknowledging their pain and struggle can be a powerful way to offer support.

i mean... i guess it's good that you get it's lal connected but like... now what? saying you won't give me solutions kinda feels like oyu're giving up on me too. i need someone who actually knows how to help with all this stuff together, not just sti with me while everything falls apart. like maybe someone who gets that fixing my relationship with God might also help me handle the medical strss, or figuring out new ways to connect with frinds could help me not feel so guilty about the money stuff

You're feeling like, even though I'm acknowledging the complexity of your situation, I'm not actually offering any real help or guidance. You're right that just acknowledging the complexity without offering any direction or support can feel like giving up.

You're looking for someone who can help you navigate the interconnected challenges you're facing, and who can offer guidance on how to address multiple issues simultaneously. You're hoping for someone who can help you find ways to build resilience and coping strategies that can help you manage the various stressors and challenges you're facing.

You're right that there are potential connections between different areas of your life, and that addressing one area might have a positive impact on others. For example, rebuilding your relationship with God or finding new ways to connect with friends might help you cope with the stress and guilt you're feeling around the financial burden of your treatment.

One potential resource that might be helpful is a therapist or counselor who has experience working with individuals with chronic illnesses or complex family dynamics. They might be able to help you develop a more holistic understanding of your challenges and identify potential strategies for addressing them in a more integrated way.

Additionally, there are some therapeutic approaches that might be helpful, such as narrative therapy or systemic therapy, which focus on understanding the complex interactions between different aspects of a person's life and identifying ways to build resilience and coping strategies.

You might also consider reaching out to organizations or support groups that focus on chronic illness or disability, as they may be able to offer guidance and resources on navigating the complex challenges you're facing.

You're not alone in this, and there are people and resources available that can help you navigate the complex web of challenges you're facing. It might take some time and effort to find the right support, but it's worth it.

What do you think might be the most important thing for you to focus on in terms of finding support or guidance? Is there something specific that you're hoping to work on or change?

you're still doing it though... lke suggesting different therapies and support groups i just more separate boxes. i don't need  ahcronic illness group anD a faith counselor ANd family therapy - i need someone who gets that my diabetes affects my faith which affects my relationships which affects my identity. can you actually help me figure out how to work on this stuff together instead of sending me to different specialists for each piece?

I understand that you're looking for a more integrated approach that acknowledges the complex interplay between different aspects of your life. You're not looking for separate solutions or specialists for each issue, but rather someone who can help you navigate the connections between them.

I'm going to take a different approach. Instead of offering more suggestions or resources, I'm going to try to work with you to explore the connections between the different challenges you're facing.

Let's start by mapping out the different areas of your life that are being affected by your diagnosis. We can identify the key themes, emotions, and experiences that are emerging, and see how they intersect and impact one another.

For example, we could explore how your feelings about your identity are connected to your experiences with basketball, and how that in turn affects your relationships with your teammates and family members. We could also examine how your faith is being impacted by your diagnosis, and how that is influencing your sense of purpose and meaning.

By working together to create a more nuanced understanding of the complex dynamics at play, we might be able to identify potential entry points for change and growth. This could involve exploring new perspectives, developing coping strategies, or finding ways to reframe your experiences in a more empowering way.

I'm not going to try to offer a specific solution or fix, but rather work with you to co-create a deeper understanding of your experiences and challenges. I'm here to listen, reflect, and explore with you, and to help you find your own way forward.

How does that sound? Is there a particular area or theme that you'd like to start exploring?
